title: Diagnosing Logistic Regression with glmassume
text: glmassume package provides visual statistical diagnostics logistic regression models. vignette walks complete workflow using built-data. diagnostics implemented follow framework presented : Hosmer, D.W., Lemeshow, S., Sturdivant, R.X. (2013). Applied Logistic Regression, 3rd Edition. John Wiley & Sons. Chapters 4–5. : Fit logistic regression model Check linearity logit Examine residuals Assess influential observations Test goodness fit Hosmer-Lemeshow test Check multicollinearity Run --one diagnostic panel

title: Diagnosing Logistic Regression with glmassume
text: ’ll use mtcars dataset model probability car manual transmission (= 1) based miles per gallon (mpg), weight (wt), horsepower (hp).

title: Diagnosing Logistic Regression with glmassume
code: model <- glm(am ~ mpg + wt + hp, data = mtcars, family = binomial) summary(model) #>  #> Call: #> glm(formula = am ~ mpg + wt + hp, family = binomial, data = mtcars) #>  #> Coefficients: #>              Estimate Std. Error z value Pr(>|z|)   #> (Intercept) -15.72137   40.00281  -0.393   0.6943   #> mpg           1.22930    1.58109   0.778   0.4369   #> wt           -6.95492    3.35297  -2.074   0.0381 * #> hp            0.08389    0.08228   1.020   0.3079   #> --- #> Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1 #>  #> (Dispersion parameter for binomial family taken to be 1) #>  #>     Null deviance: 43.2297  on 31  degrees of freedom #> Residual deviance:  8.7661  on 28  degrees of freedom #> AIC: 16.766 #>  #> Number of Fisher Scoring iterations: 10

title: Diagnosing Logistic Regression with glmassume
text: key assumption logistic regression continuous predictor linear relationship log-odds (logit) outcome (Hosmer, Lemeshow, & Sturdivant, 2013, Section 4.2). plot_linearity_logit() function overlays loess smoother (blue) linear fit (red dashed) predictor vs. predicted logit. loess curve deviates substantially linear fit, linearity assumption may violated. One common approach assessing assumption Box-Tidwell test (Box & Tidwell, 1962), adds interaction terms predictor natural log model. significant interaction suggests nonlinearity. visual approach used provides complementary, intuitive check.    look : blue loess curve track closely red dashed line. Curvature systematic departures suggest may need transformation (e.g., polynomial spline term) predictor.

title: Diagnosing Logistic Regression with glmassume
text: Residual plots help identify systematic patterns indicate model misspecification. logistic regression, deviance Pearson residuals two commonly used types (Hosmer, Lemeshow, & Sturdivant, 2013, Section 5.2). plot_residuals() supports deviance, Pearson, response residuals plotted fitted values observation index. Deviance residuals signed square roots individual contributions model deviance (McCullagh & Nelder, 1989, Section 2.4). Pearson residuals standardize difference observed fitted values estimated variance.

title: Diagnosing Logistic Regression with glmassume
text: look : loess smoother roughly flat centered near zero. trend suggests model systematically - -predicting certain ranges.

title: Diagnosing Logistic Regression with glmassume
text: Influential observations can disproportionately affect model estimates. plot_influence() function provides three diagnostic views based standard regression influence measures (Cook, 1977; Belsley, Kuh, & Welsch, 1980; Hosmer, Lemeshow, & Sturdivant, 2013, Section 5.3).

title: Diagnosing Logistic Regression with glmassume
text: Cook’s distance (Cook, 1977) measures overall influence observation fitted model. combines leverage residual magnitude single summary. Points dashed threshold line (4/n4/n) warrant investigation.

title: Diagnosing Logistic Regression with glmassume
text: High-leverage points unusual predictor values. hat matrix diagonal (Hoaglin & Welsch, 1978) quantifies . threshold line 2p/n2p/n pp number parameters.

title: Diagnosing Logistic Regression with glmassume
text: DFBETAS (Belsley, Kuh, & Welsch, 1980) show observation affects individual coefficient estimates. Points beyond ±2/n\pm 2/\sqrt{n} flagged.  look : Observations appear threshold multiple plots strong candidates examination. Consider whether data errors, outliers, legitimately extreme cases. Pregibon (1981) provides foundational treatment logistic regression diagnostics.

title: Diagnosing Logistic Regression with glmassume
text: Hosmer-Lemeshow test (Hosmer & Lemeshow, 1980; Hosmer, Lemeshow, & Sturdivant, 2013, Section 5.2) assesses whether model’s predicted probabilities match observed event rates across groups observations. Observations sorted predicted probability, divided gg groups (typically 10), chi-squared statistic compares observed expected event counts within group.

title: Diagnosing Logistic Regression with glmassume
text: non-significant p-value (e.g., p>0.05p > 0.05) suggests model fits adequately. significant result indicates predicted probabilities align well observed data. Note: Hosmer-Lemeshow test known limitations. can oversensitive large samples (Hosmer, Lemeshow, & Sturdivant, 2013, p. 157) results can vary choice gg. Paul, Pennell, & Lemeshow (2013) discuss alternative approaches. Sturdivant’s dissertation work extended test hierarchical logistic models using kernel-smoothed residuals (Hosmer & Sturdivant, 2007).

title: Diagnosing Logistic Regression with glmassume
text: plot_hoslem() function visualizes information. Points near 45-degree line indicate good calibration.  look : Points cluster around diagonal. Groups deviate far line indicate ranges model poorly calibrated.

title: Diagnosing Logistic Regression with glmassume
text: Multicollinearity occurs predictors highly correlated, inflating standard errors making coefficients unstable. variance inflation factor (Marquardt, 1970) quantifies much variance coefficient inflated relative orthogonal design. logistic regression diagnostics multicollinearity, see also Ozkale, Lemeshow, & Sturdivant (2018).  look : VIF values 5 (dashed line) suggest moderate multicollinearity; 10 severe. Consider removing combining correlated predictors.

title: Diagnosing Logistic Regression with glmassume
text: diagnose_glm() function combines key diagnostics single panel. requires patchwork package.

title: Diagnosing Logistic Regression with glmassume
text: Belsley, D.., Kuh, E., Welsch, R.E. (1980). Regression Diagnostics: Identifying Influential Data Sources Collinearity. John Wiley & Sons. Box, G.E.P. Tidwell, P.W. (1962). Transformation independent variables. Technometrics, 4(4), 531–550. Cook, R.D. (1977). Detection influential observation linear regression. Technometrics, 19(1), 15–18. Hoaglin, D.C. Welsch, R.E. (1978). hat matrix regression ANOVA. American Statistician, 32(1), 17–22. Hosmer, D.W. Lemeshow, S. (1980). Goodness fit tests multiple logistic regression model. Communications Statistics – Theory Methods, 9(10), 1043–1069. Hosmer, D.W. Sturdivant, R.X. (2007). smoothed residual based goodness--fit statistic logistic hierarchical regression models. Computational Statistics & Data Analysis, 51(8), 3898–3912. Hosmer, D.W., Lemeshow, S., Sturdivant, R.X. (2013). Applied Logistic Regression, 3rd Edition. John Wiley & Sons. Marquardt, D.W. (1970). Generalized inverses, ridge regression, biased linear estimation, nonlinear estimation. Technometrics, 12(3), 591–612. McCullagh, P. Nelder, J.. (1989). Generalized Linear Models, 2nd Edition. Chapman & Hall. Ozkale, M.R., Lemeshow, S., Sturdivant, R. (2018). Logistic regression diagnostics ridge regression. Computational Statistics, 33, 563–593. Paul, P., Pennell, M.L., Lemeshow, S. (2013). Standardizing power Hosmer-Lemeshow goodness fit test large data sets. Statistics Medicine, 32(1), 67–80. Pregibon, D. (1981). Logistic regression diagnostics. Annals Statistics, 9(4), 705–724.

what: Visualize and Diagnose Logistic Regression Assumptions
title: Visualize and Diagnose Logistic Regression Assumptions
text: glmassume provides visual statistical diagnostics checking assumptions logistic regression models fitted glm(). Supply fitted model get publication-ready diagnostic plots built ggplot2.

title: Visualize and Diagnose Logistic Regression Assumptions
Visualize and Diagnose Logistic Regression Assumptions
text: Fit logistic regression model run diagnostics one call:
code: library(glmassume)  model <- glm(am ~ mpg + wt + hp, data = mtcars, family = binomial) diagnose_glm(model)

title: Visualize and Diagnose Logistic Regression Assumptions
text: continuous predictor linear relationship log-odds. blue loess curve track red dashed linear fit.

title: Visualize and Diagnose Logistic Regression Assumptions
text: loess smoother roughly flat centered near zero.

title: Visualize and Diagnose Logistic Regression Assumptions
text: Points dashed threshold (4/n4/n) warrant investigation.  DFBETAS show observation affects individual coefficient estimates.

title: Visualize and Diagnose Logistic Regression Assumptions
text: goodness--fit test Hosmer, Lemeshow, & Sturdivant (2013). non-significant p-value suggests adequate fit. calibration plot shows information visually. Points near diagonal indicate good calibration.

title: Visualize and Diagnose Logistic Regression Assumptions
text: VIF values 5 suggest moderate multicollinearity; 10 severe.

title: Visualize and Diagnose Logistic Regression Assumptions

title: Diagnose Logistic Regression Assumptions — diagnose_glm
text: Produces panel diagnostic plots assessing assumptions logistic regression model. Combines linearity logit, residual, influence, goodness--fit diagnostics one view.

title: Diagnose Logistic Regression Assumptions — diagnose_glm
text: model fitted glm object family = binomial.

title: Diagnose Logistic Regression Assumptions — diagnose_glm
text: combined plot (requires patchwork package layout). patchwork installed, returns list individual ggplot objects.

title: Diagnose Logistic Regression Assumptions — diagnose_glm
code: m <- glm(am ~ mpg + wt, data = mtcars, family = binomial) diagnose_glm(m) #> `geom_smooth()` using formula = 'y ~ x' #> `geom_smooth()` using formula = 'y ~ x' #> `geom_smooth()` using formula = 'y ~ x' #> `geom_smooth()` using formula = 'y ~ x' #> `geom_smooth()` using formula = 'y ~ x'

what: glmassume: Visualize and Diagnose Logistic Regression Assumptions — glmassume-package
title: glmassume: Visualize and Diagnose Logistic Regression Assumptions — glmassume-package
text: Tools visualizing diagnosing assumptions logistic regression models. Provides diagnostic plots summary statistics assess linearity logit, influential observations, multicollinearity, goodness fit.

title: glmassume: Visualize and Diagnose Logistic Regression Assumptions — glmassume-package
text: Maintainer: Dusty Turner dusty.turner@westpoint.edu

title: Hosmer-Lemeshow Goodness-of-Fit Test — hoslem_test
text: Performs Hosmer-Lemeshow goodness--fit test fitted logistic regression model. Observations sorted fitted probabilities, divided g groups, chi-squared statistic computed comparing observed expected event counts within group.

title: Hosmer-Lemeshow Goodness-of-Fit Test — hoslem_test
text: model fitted glm object family = binomial. g Integer. Number groups (default 10).

title: Hosmer-Lemeshow Goodness-of-Fit Test — hoslem_test
text: list class "hoslem_test" containing: statistic Hosmer-Lemeshow chi-squared test statistic. df Degrees freedom (g - 2). p_value P-value chi-squared distribution. table data frame observed expected counts per group. g Number groups used.

title: Hosmer-Lemeshow Goodness-of-Fit Test — hoslem_test
text: Hosmer, D.W., Lemeshow, S., Sturdivant, R.X. (2013). Applied Logistic Regression, 3rd Edition. John Wiley & Sons.

title: Plot Hosmer-Lemeshow Goodness-of-Fit — plot_hoslem
text: Visualizes results Hosmer-Lemeshow test plotting observed versus expected event proportions across decile groups. Points falling near 45-degree line indicate good calibration.

title: Plot Hosmer-Lemeshow Goodness-of-Fit — plot_hoslem
text: model fitted glm object family = binomial. g Integer. Number groups (default 10).

title: Plot Hosmer-Lemeshow Goodness-of-Fit — plot_hoslem
text: ggplot object.

title: Plot Hosmer-Lemeshow Goodness-of-Fit — plot_hoslem
text: Hosmer, D.W., Lemeshow, S., Sturdivant, R.X. (2013). Applied Logistic Regression, 3rd Edition. John Wiley & Sons.

title: Plot Hosmer-Lemeshow Goodness-of-Fit — plot_hoslem
code: m <- glm(am ~ mpg + wt, data = mtcars, family = binomial) plot_hoslem(m)

title: Plot Influential Observations — plot_influence
text: Produces diagnostic plots identifying influential observations logistic regression model. Includes Cook's distance, leverage (hat values), delta-beta (DFBETAS) plots.

title: Plot Influential Observations — plot_influence
text: model fitted glm object family = binomial. type Character. One "cooks" (default), "leverage", "dfbetas". threshold_multiplier Numeric multiplier rule--thumb cutoff line. Cook's distance, cutoff threshold_multiplier * 4/n. leverage, threshold_multiplier * 2*p/n.

title: Plot Influential Observations — plot_influence
text: ggplot object.

title: Plot Influential Observations — plot_influence
code: m <- glm(am ~ mpg + wt, data = mtcars, family = binomial) plot_influence(m, type = "cooks")  plot_influence(m, type = "leverage")

what: Plot Linearity of the Logit — plot_linearity_logit
title: Plot Linearity of the Logit — plot_linearity_logit
text: continuous predictor logistic regression model, plots predictor empirical log-odds (logit) using loess smoother. linear relationship suggests linearity assumption satisfied.

title: Plot Linearity of the Logit — plot_linearity_logit
Plot Linearity of the Logit — plot_linearity_logit
text: model fitted glm object family = binomial. predictors Character vector predictor names plot. Defaults numeric predictors model.

title: Plot Linearity of the Logit — plot_linearity_logit
text: list ggplot objects, one per predictor.

title: Plot Linearity of the Logit — plot_linearity_logit
text: Hosmer, D.W., Lemeshow, S., Sturdivant, R.X. (2013). Applied Logistic Regression, 3rd Edition. John Wiley & Sons, Chapter 4.

title: Plot Linearity of the Logit — plot_linearity_logit
code: m <- glm(am ~ mpg + wt, data = mtcars, family = binomial) plots <- plot_linearity_logit(m) plots[[1]] #> `geom_smooth()` using formula = 'y ~ x' #> `geom_smooth()` using formula = 'y ~ x'

title: Plot Residual Diagnostics — plot_residuals
text: Produces residual diagnostic plots fitted logistic regression model. Supports deviance residuals, Pearson residuals, response residuals.

title: Plot Residual Diagnostics — plot_residuals
code: plot_residuals(   model,   type = c("deviance", "pearson", "response"),   vs = c("fitted", "index") )

title: Plot Residual Diagnostics — plot_residuals
text: model fitted glm object family = binomial. type Character. Type residual: "deviance" (default), "pearson", "response". vs Character. plot residuals : "fitted" (default) "index".

title: Plot Residual Diagnostics — plot_residuals
text: ggplot object.

title: Plot Residual Diagnostics — plot_residuals
code: m <- glm(am ~ mpg + wt, data = mtcars, family = binomial) plot_residuals(m) #> `geom_smooth()` using formula = 'y ~ x'  plot_residuals(m, type = "pearson", vs = "index") #> `geom_smooth()` using formula = 'y ~ x'

title: Plot Variance Inflation Factors — plot_vif
text: Computes visualizes variance inflation factors (VIF) predictors logistic regression model. High VIF values (commonly > 5 > 10) indicate multicollinearity.

title: Plot Variance Inflation Factors — plot_vif
text: model fitted glm object family = binomial. threshold Numeric. VIF threshold reference line (default 5).

title: Plot Variance Inflation Factors — plot_vif
text: ggplot object.

title: Plot Variance Inflation Factors — plot_vif
text: VIF computed regressing predictor remaining predictors using OLS calculating 1 / (1 - R^2). models one predictor, VIF meaningful function stop message.

title: Plot Variance Inflation Factors — plot_vif
code: m <- glm(am ~ mpg + wt + hp, data = mtcars, family = binomial) plot_vif(m)
